Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't rely on other people

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I try hard to control my emotions
I dwell on how I used to be before the event

issues.item: Nonverbal Memory: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
issues.explanation: This directly conflicts with the selected Verbal Access item, which states the patient can talk about what happened very easily. These two memory items describe opposite clinical states and are not coherent together.

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This directly conflicts with the selected Nonverbal Memory item, which says the event cannot be put into words. These are opposing descriptions of the same memory process.

vignette:
The patient is a 79-year-old North American non-binary widowed adult who presents with persistent post-traumatic distress following a medical trauma. Their PCL-5 score of 35 is consistent with clinically significant symptoms that continue to intrude into daily life. They describe sudden moments of feeling as though the frightening event is happening again, accompanied by vivid picture-like memories and occasional auditory fragments of what was heard at the time. At times, these experiences feel immediate and overwhelming, and they are often followed by feeling superalert, jumpy, or on guard.

Several everyday cues intensify this pattern, particularly confrontation, being looked at sexually, and comments about sexual violence. In those moments, the patient becomes more watchful and distressed, and the event is harder to keep at a distance in memory. They also report a growing mistrust of other people, a sense that others cannot be relied upon, and periods of feeling emotionally dead inside. These appraisals sit heavily with them and colour how they make sense of present-day interactions.

In response, they try hard to control their emotions, tend to look down, and spend time dwelling on how they used to be before the trauma. They also report that the memories themselves can intensify the sense of current threat and feed further mistrust. The same remembered images and inner sounds seem to keep the experience alive in the present, making it harder to feel settled or safe.

Overall, the presentation reflects a cycle in which reminders and trauma memories repeatedly sharpen their sense of danger, strengthen negative views of others and the self, and leave them emotionally constricted. Their coping style appears organized around keeping feelings contained and reducing exposure to social contact, yet this has come at the cost of greater isolation and a diminished sense of continuity with the person they once were.
